name.text: Week 14 – Q2: Wave Parameters and Reflection at Media Boundary

questiontext.text:
Scenario: Your company is designing a {@f_GHz@} GHz radar sensor for an autonomous vehicle. The radar signal must pass through the vehicle's bumper to detect objects outside. The signal propagates from the sensor housing (Medium 1, \(\varepsilon_{r1} = {@eps_r1@}\)) through the bumper material (Medium 2, \(\varepsilon_{r2} = {@eps_r2@}\)) at normal incidence. Both materials are non-magnetic and lossless at this frequency.

Your job: determine the wave parameters and quantify how much radar signal is lost to reflection at the housing-bumper boundary.

Part A — Wave Parameters in the Sensor Housing (Medium 1)

(a) Phase velocity in Medium 1: \(v_{p1} = \) [[input:ans1]] [[validation:ans1]] m/s

Enter in m/s, e.g., 1.23e8. Scientific notation is accepted.

(b) Wavelength in Medium 1: \(\lambda_1 = \) [[input:ans2]] [[validation:ans2]] m

Enter in metres, e.g., 0.234.

(c) Wavenumber in Medium 1: \(k_1 = 2\pi/\lambda_1 = \) [[input:ans3]] [[validation:ans3]] rad/m

Enter in rad/m, e.g., 31.4.

Part B — Impedance Mismatch and Reflection

(d) Intrinsic impedance of Medium 1 (sensor housing): \(\eta_1 = \) [[input:ans4]] [[validation:ans4]] \(\Omega\)

Enter in ohms, e.g., 42.0.

(e) Intrinsic impedance of Medium 2 (bumper): \(\eta_2 = \) [[input:ans5]] [[validation:ans5]] \(\Omega\)

Enter in ohms, e.g., 42.0.

(f) Reflection coefficient at the boundary (wave travelling from Medium 1 into Medium 2): \(\Gamma = \) [[input:ans6]] [[validation:ans6]]

The sign matters — \(\Gamma\) can be negative, e.g., -0.52.

(g) What fraction of the incident power is reflected? \(|\Gamma|^2 = \) [[input:ans7]] [[validation:ans7]]

Enter a number between 0 and 1, e.g., 0.29.

Part C — Signal Budget and Design Insight

(h) What fraction of the incident power is transmitted into the bumper? (Energy conservation: reflected + transmitted = 1.)

Transmitted power fraction = [[input:ans8]] [[validation:ans8]]

Enter a number between 0 and 1, e.g., 0.29.

(i) If the bumper were redesigned so that \(\varepsilon_{r2} = \varepsilon_{r1}\) (same permittivity as the sensor housing), what would happen to the reflected power?

[[input:ans9]] [[validation:ans9]]

(j) If the bumper's permittivity \(\varepsilon_{r2}\) is increased (while the sensor housing remains unchanged), what happens to the fraction of reflected power?

[[input:ans10]] [[validation:ans10]]

generalfeedback.text:
Worked Solution

Given: \(f = {@f_GHz@}\) GHz, \(\varepsilon_{r1} = {@eps_r1@}\), \(\varepsilon_{r2} = {@eps_r2@}\), both non-magnetic and lossless.

(a) \(v_{p1} = c/\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r1}} = {@tans1@}\) m/s

(b) \(\lambda_1 = v_{p1}/f = {@tans2@}\) m

(c) \(k_1 = 2\pi/\lambda_1 = {@tans3@}\) rad/m

(d) \(\eta_1 = 120\pi/\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r1}} = {@tans4@}\;\Omega\)

(e) \(\eta_2 = 120\pi/\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r2}} = {@tans5@}\;\Omega\)

(f) \(\Gamma = (\eta_2 - \eta_1)/(\eta_2 + \eta_1) = {@tans6@}\). Negative because \(\varepsilon_{r2} > \varepsilon_{r1}\) makes \(\eta_2 < \eta_1\).

(g) \(|\Gamma|^2 = {@tans7@}\) ({@float(tans7*100)@}% reflected, {@float((1-tans7)*100)@}% transmitted).

(h) Transmitted fraction \(= 1 - |\Gamma|^2 = 1 - {@tans7@} = {@tans8@}\) ({@float(tans8*100)@}% of the radar power reaches the bumper).

Note: In a real bumper, the signal crosses two boundaries (entering and exiting). Each boundary reflects some power, so the total transmission is less than this single-boundary result. The full analysis requires multiple-reflection theory.

(i) When \(\varepsilon_{r2} = \varepsilon_{r1}\), both media have the same intrinsic impedance: \(\eta_2 = \eta_1\). Then \(\Gamma = (\eta_1 - \eta_1)/(\eta_1 + \eta_1) = 0\). No reflection occurs — the boundary is invisible to the wave. This is impedance matching, the same principle used in transmission line design (recall the quarter-wave transformer from Week 13).

(j) As \(\varepsilon_{r2}\) increases (with \(\varepsilon_{r1}\) fixed), \(\eta_2 = \eta_0/\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r2}}\) decreases, increasing the impedance mismatch \(|\eta_2 - \eta_1|\). This makes \(|\Gamma|\) larger, so more power is reflected. In the extreme (\(\varepsilon_{r2} \to \infty\)), \(\eta_2 \to 0\) and \(\Gamma \to -1\) — total reflection, like hitting a perfect conductor. This is why bumper material selection matters for radar performance.

questionnote.text: {@sconcat("f=", f_GHz, "GHz er1=", eps_r1, " er2=", eps_r2, " Gamma=", tans6, " Ptrans=", tans8)@}

feedbackvariables.text: vp_err_flag: if is(eps_r1 > 1) and is(abs(vp1_err) > 0) and is(abs(ans1 - vp1_err)/abs(vp1_err) < 0.06) then 1 else 0;

truefeedback.text: Correct formula applied to your \(\eta\) values — half credit (your \(\eta\) values were wrong).

feedbackvariables.text: abs_err: if is(abs(Gamma_abs) > 0.001) and is(abs(ans7 - Gamma_abs)/abs(Gamma_abs) < 0.06) then 1 else 0;

truefeedback.text: Correct. {@float(tans8*100)@}% of the radar power is transmitted into the bumper.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Transmitted fraction = \(1 - |\Gamma|^2\). Energy conservation: reflected + transmitted = 1.

truefeedback.text: Correct. When \(\varepsilon_{r2} = \varepsilon_{r1}\), both media have the same impedance, so \(\Gamma = 0\). This is impedance matching — the same principle behind the quarter-wave transformer from Week 13.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. When both media have the same \(\varepsilon_r\), they have the same \(\eta\), so \(\Gamma = (\eta_1 - \eta_1)/(\eta_1 + \eta_1) = 0\). No mismatch means no reflection.

truefeedback.text: Correct. Increasing \(\varepsilon_{r2}\) lowers \(\eta_2\), increasing the impedance mismatch and reflection. In the extreme, \(\Gamma \to -1\) — total reflection, as from a perfect conductor.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. As \(\varepsilon_{r2}\) increases, \(\eta_2 = \eta_0/\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r2}}\) decreases, making \(|\eta_2 - \eta_1|\) larger. Larger mismatch = more reflection.

hint.text: Wave parameters: In a dielectric medium, the wave slows down: \(v_p = c/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}\). The wavelength changes with velocity (\(\lambda = v_p/f\)), but frequency stays the same across any boundary. The intrinsic impedance \(\eta = 120\pi/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}\) tells you how much resistance the medium offers to the wave — this is what determines reflection.

Reflection at a boundary: The reflection coefficient has the same form as the transmission line reflection from Week 13: \(\Gamma = (\eta_2 - \eta_1)/(\eta_2 + \eta_1)\). The sign matters: if Medium 2 has higher \(\varepsilon_r\), it has lower \(\eta\), making \(\Gamma\) negative. A negative \(\Gamma\) means the reflected electric field flips direction.

Power and design insight: Reflected power = \(|\Gamma|^2\), transmitted = \(1 - |\Gamma|^2\) (energy conservation). For the conceptual parts: think about what happens when both media are identical — if there is no boundary mismatch, there should be no reflection. This is impedance matching.
